title: The Macro Minute: Is it time to book gains?

Happy Tuesday out there team 42 your
skipper here at Darius D to present our
macro minute for Tuesday October 14th
2025. Hope everyone's having a great
week. So as always we'll start with the
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
or to get on the right side of market
risk to the extent you haven't been
following Kiss or Dr. Mo need to be a
client of 40 macro. So today's Kimacro
question is is it time to book gains?
Our answer, resurgent trade tensions are
contributing to significant declines
across risk assets while fueling a
flight to safety into gold, the asset we
identified as having the best defensive
properties from a portfolio construction
perspective a year ago. China escalated
its maritime power dispute with the US
by sanctioning the American units of a
South Korean shipping conglomerate and
threatening further retaliation.
Although we don't have any more edge in
the next investor regarding how this
latest flare up will end, we do maintain
confidence that the administration is
pursuing bullish paradigm C and is
highly unlikely to pivot back to bearish
paradigm B, the cut phase of the cut
growth sequence required to address the
geopolitically driven supply and demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market
that we identified in our inaugural
investing during regime presentation in
the summer of 2023.
Regarding the probability of a rural
phase transition, Federal Reserve Chair
Jerome Pile and other official speeches
today may reshape monetary policy
expectations amid a US government
shutdown, sparse economic data, and
resurgent trade frictions. That the
September NFIB small business optimism
survey data clearly supported our sticky
inflation theme and tariff pig and the
inflation python thesis is concerning.
Is it finally time for a healthy 5 to 8%
pullback and or will asset markets
devolve into a full-blown rural phase
transition soon? And for those who may
be unfamiliar, royal phase just means
going from a risk-on regime to a riskoff
regime or vice versa. Terms of the
answer to that question, I don't know.
And I do not live in fear of not knowing
the answer because I know KISS and Dr.
Mo have our backs because they are as
optimized for minimizing downside
capture in bare markets as they are for
maximizing upside capture in bull
markets. They are however not optimized
for freaking out about 5 to 8% pullbacks
in the S&P 500 or short-term
liquidations in the crypto market. If
successfully trading every wiggle in
global financial markets is what you
desire, then best of luck on your
journey to retire on time and
comfortably because you're definitely
going to need it. All told, bank
earnings were supportive of our paradigm
C theme as sales and earnings beats
continue to roll in just as we predicted
back in April. Focusing on the fiscal
stimulus plus monetary easing plus
substantial deregulation forest instead
of the tariff trees has helped us
maintain confidence in our 100%ile
bullish relative to global Wall Street
paradigm C theme since April and we
continue to view dip buying as the most
appropriate strategy. If we're wrong on
this view then Kiss and Dr. Mo will
appropriately adjust our positioning
faster than I ever could as the
fundamental bull and architect of our
paradigm C theme. Anyway, please read
the previous sentence two to three times
so that you can fully comprehend the
value of our highly differentiated macro
risk management process. I'm not going
to read it two, three times, but I'm
going to read it one more time. If we're
wrong on this view, Kiss and Dr. Mo will
appropriately adjust our positioning
faster than I ever could as a
fundamental bull and architect of our
paradigm C theme. Anyway, talked about
this at nauseium all year. It's about
having your institutional grade risk
management process separate from your
institutional grade fundamental research
process. Your fundamental research
process is designed to help you
understand the full distribution of
probable economic market and policy
outcomes. And you use the risk
management signals to, you know,
actually position and reposition for
those outcomes as the evolution and
shape of the distribution evolve as we
move forward in time. You're not always
going to be in the same place in the
growth cycle. You're not always going to
be in the same place in the inflation
cycle. You're not always going to be in
the same place in the in the fiscal
policy cycle. You're not always going to
be in the same place in the monetary
policy cycle. You're not always going to
be in the same place in the liquidity
cycle. You're not always going to be in
the same place in position cycle. The
cycles move. You we move with the
passage of time where we are on those
sign curves moves. You know, I have this
saying, you know, as a former football
player, as a former offensive lineman,
you know, when we doing walkthrough
practices, we have these upside down,
you know, big trash cans where we line
up the defensive lineman where they're
supposed to be and the linebackers and
safeties. And I had this saying, hey,
look, when the game starts, the trash
cans move. You know, you have to take
the proper blocking angles in in
practice. You can't just walk straight
to the trash can because the trash can's
got to move. Take the proper angles in
in in the markets and and or sorry, in
in practice, in the walkthrough as you
would in in a game and and translating
that back to financial markets, you
know, the these cycles, these kicks, the
six key macro cycles that matter to
asset markets, they move. And so it's
it's not our job to, you know, use our
puny little human brains, especially in
an era of AI, to try to figure out how
to best position for them. It's our job
to allow the market to tell us when to
change our positioning to best position
for them. And that's what Kiss and Dr.
Mo do. Kiss for retail investors, Dr. Mo
for institutional investors. So, uh,

market and the dollar obviously both

datadriven research framework that we
borrow that we you know we staying on on

uh Neil How and his late colleague Bill

standpoint. So uh really proud of what
we built there and obviously it's helped
us tremendously in terms of keeping our
clients on the right side of market
risk. Uh and then uh lastly
transitioning to um transition to our uh
October macro scouting report to answer
the first part of the question. What
specifically are we looking at in our
research to observe evidence of labor
hoarding? Uh so there's two specific
things that we're look sorry three
technically three specific things that
we're looking at. One is the uh
breakdown in the historical relationship
between the private sector highest rate
uh and the private sector quits rate. So
the blue line in this chart here shows
the private sector highs rate which is
now at a well below trend level of 3.5%.
The private sector crits rate which has
historically been inversely correlated
to this u um time series has not broken
out to an above trend level still at a
well below trend level of 1.2%. So even
though companies are no longer hiring
people they're not on they're not doing
what they used to do which is we fire
people when we stop hiring. Right now
they're just not hiring. So that's a
breakdown in that relationship. That's
evidence of labor hoarding. we have a
breakdown in the uh correlation between
household survey total employment and
Joe's total job openings for the first
time this year those time series as well
you know we've seen a continued uh
expansion in household survey total
employment but we've seen a significant
decline in Jot's total job openings i.e
you know marginal labor demand as
evidenced by that survey. That's the
first time in the history of the survey
where we've seen a divergence between
these two um two time series something
we've been calling out for the past
three years now in the context of our
resilient US economy theme. And then
finally uh the last thing we're looking
at in terms of evidence of the labor
hoarding thesis is uh if you look at the
breakdown between uh the conference
board labor survey differential and the
U3 unemployment rate and right now the
conference board labor survey
differential has crashed all the way
from a peak of 47.1 to a value of 7.8.
uh and if you and it's obviously been
very correlated to uh the labor market
or to the U3 headline unemployment rate
uh for you know as far back as we have
the data you know which goes back to the
mid1 1960s. So if the but you've seen
this big divergence between how the
unemployment rate has performed relative
to how this time series has if this time
series if the relationship was as as
tight as it used to be and had been for
you know 50 60 years then the
unemployment rate would be somewhere
around 6%.
So we've seen this significant
degradation in the labor market both
from the perspective of consumers but
also from the perspective of corporates
but we're not necessarily seeing it show
up in the aggregate you know discharge
layoffs and discharge and unlim
statistics and in our opinion we think
that's plenty evidence of labor hoarding
and our labor hoarding thesis has
supported our resilient US economy theme
which we authored in September of 2022.
Um so uh hopefully everyone's aware of
that. So we'll wrap it up there. Jerry
